seo.title: Home Inspection FAQs MA | Ken Arnold Home Inspection
seo.description: Answers to common questions about home inspections in Massachusetts — cost, duration, scope, radon, well water, and what happens after. MA Lic. #587.

sections.content_class: relative container-edge container-edge--wide py-12 md:py-[72px] lg:py-[100px]
sections.body:
21 Questions, Answered Straight

Home Inspection FAQs Massachusetts Buyers & Sellers

Drawn from 22 years of fielding the same questions from buyers, sellers, agents, and investors across the Pioneer Valley. Search below or jump to a category — every question is here.

sections.html: Cost & Pricing 1 Process & Scope 7 Specialty Testing 4 Scheduling & Conditions 3 After the Inspection 3 Working with Ken 3

Pricing

Cost & Pricing

What an Inspection Actually Costs

For a typical Pioneer Valley single-family home, expect $600–$750 depending on size. Condos run $650, two-families $650–$800, and three- or four-family properties $800–$1,100. Specialty add-ons (radon, well water, septic, termite/WDI, mold, thermal imaging) range from $95 to $425 each when bundled with the inspection. See the full pricing page for details.
Inspection Day

Process & Scope

What Happens, What's Covered, and How Long It Takes

All major systems: roof, structure (foundation, framing), exterior (siding, windows, grading), electrical (service, panel, wiring sample, GFCI/AFCI), plumbing (supply, drains, water heater), HVAC (heating, cooling, ventilation), insulation, interior surfaces, built-in appliances, and accessible attic, basement, and crawlspace areas. Inspections follow the ASHI Standards of Practice and Massachusetts licensing requirements.
Most single-family inspections in the Pioneer Valley run 2.5–3 hours on-site. Older New England homes — especially with additions, fieldstone foundations, or knob-and-tube wiring — often take closer to 4 hours. Condos run 1.5–2 hours, multi-family properties 4–6 hours.
Yes — strongly recommended. Walking the property with the inspector is one of the most educational parts of buying a home. You'll see findings firsthand, learn how the systems work, and get answers to questions in real time. Most buyers find this more valuable than the report itself.
Ken delivers your photo-documented digital report promptly after the inspection — organized by safety priority so you and your agent can act inside your due-diligence window.
Specialty testing (radon, well water, septic, termite/WDI, mold, lead, asbestos), code compliance, septic system condition, building permit history, environmental hazards beyond what's visually apparent, and warranty work. These are either separate specialty services or work for licensed specialists in those fields.
A home inspection (ASHI standards) is a visual and operational evaluation of major systems, focused on identifying issues that affect the buyer's purchase decision. A code inspection — performed by a town inspector — checks compliance with the building code at a specific construction milestone. They serve different purposes; passing a code inspection doesn't mean a home is defect-free.
Often, yes. Identifying and addressing issues before listing reduces negotiation risk during contingency, lets you fix issues at contractor rates you choose, and can shorten time-on-market in competitive segments. More on pre-listing inspection.
Add-Ons

Specialty Testing

Radon, Well Water, Septic, Lead

Yes — the EPA recommends radon testing on every Massachusetts real-estate transaction, and the Pioneer Valley has documented elevated levels in many municipalities (especially Greenfield, Holyoke, and Sunderland). A 48-hour test is $125 as an add-on. More on radon testing.
If the property is on a private well — common throughout rural Pioneer Valley — yes. Standard panels test for bacteria, lead, nitrates, iron, manganese, and other common contaminants. Many lenders (FHA, VA) require it. More on well water testing.
A Title 5 inspection is a state-mandated formal septic inspection required for almost all property transfers in Massachusetts. It must be performed by a state-certified Title 5 inspector. Ken's septic evaluation is a separate visual check — useful as pre-purchase due diligence — that complements the Title 5 but doesn't replace it. More on septic inspection.
Massachusetts requires lead-paint disclosure on pre-1978 housing. The home inspection identifies suspected lead-based paint (any deteriorated paint on a pre-1978 home should be assumed lead unless tested) but isn't a formal lead inspection. Formal lead inspection is performed by a state-certified Lead Inspector. Same situation for asbestos — visual identification only; formal testing requires a separate certification.
Logistics

Scheduling & Conditions

When to Book, Weather, Winter Inspections

3–7 days is typical. Time-sensitive bookings before closing deadlines can usually be accommodated — call Ken directly. Pre-listing inspections are best scheduled 3–6 weeks before listing to allow time for any repairs.
Yes — winter is actually peak season for thermal imaging, which depends on heated/unheated temperature differential to find missing insulation. Some items can't be tested in winter (running A/C, exterior hose bibs that have been winterized) and these get noted in the report.
Inspectors don't walk roofs in unsafe conditions — that's industry standard. Snow, ice, steep slope, or compromised structural condition all warrant ground-level evaluation with binoculars and drone where appropriate. Items that can't be inspected are clearly noted in the report; many buyers schedule a follow-up roof evaluation in better weather.
Findings

After the Inspection

Negotiation, Contingencies, Reusing Reports

Major findings become negotiation points. Common outcomes: the seller agrees to repair specific items before closing, the seller offers a credit or price reduction, or the buyer walks away under the inspection contingency. Discuss findings with your agent — they handle the negotiation side; the inspection report is the factual basis.
It depends on your contract. The standard Massachusetts purchase-and-sale agreement includes an inspection contingency that lets you exit the contract within a defined window (typically 7–10 days from accepted offer). Specific terms — including how findings are handled — are in your contract. Talk to your agent.
Sometimes, but with caveats. Conditions can change between inspections (e.g., a winter inspection misses summer cooling issues), the report is owned by the buyer who paid for it, and most lenders won't accept a report not commissioned by the current buyer. In practice, plan to commission your own inspection.
Trust & Independence

Working with Ken

Licensing, Referrals, Independence

Yes. Massachusetts requires home inspectors to be licensed by the Division of Professional Licensure. Ken Arnold holds Massachusetts Home Inspector License #587 and has been licensed continuously since 2004. Always verify a Massachusetts inspector's license before hiring.
Some can; we keep that boundary deliberately separate. Recommending contractors who pay referral fees (or who are part of preferred-vendor programs) is a conflict of interest. We can refer to qualified specialists for further evaluation but accept no kickbacks.
We work with the buyer or seller — not the agent. Agent referrals are fine and common, but the inspection client is the buyer (or seller) and the report is calibrated to their interest. We don't accept referral fees from agents and aren't part of any preferred-vendor program.

No questions matched

Try a different keyword, or call Ken directly — he's happy to answer questions you don't see here.

Call (413) 522-8004

sections.heading: Question Not Answered Here?
sections.body: 22 years of inspections, every question welcome. Call Ken directly or schedule online — detailed digital reports and no conflicts of interest.
sections.footnote: Licensed Home Inspector MA #587 · 35 Keets Rd, Deerfield, MA 01342 · Serving Franklin County, Hampshire County & the Pioneer Valley
